name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
فَذَرْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يُلَـٰقُوا۟ يَوْمَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ يُصْعَقُونَ (٤٥)
```

```english
So leave them until they encounter the day when they will be thunderstruck, (45)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٤٦)
```

```english
the day when their guile will not avail them in any way, nor will they be helped. (46)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ عَذَابًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٧)
```

```english
Indeed there is a punishment besides that for those who do wrong, but most of them do not know. (47)
```

```arabic
وَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنَا ۖ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ (٤٨)
```

```english
So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, for indeed you fare before Our eyes. And celebrate the praise of your Lord when you rise [at dawn], (48)
```

```arabic
وَمِنَ ٱلَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَإِدْبَـٰرَ ٱلنُّجُومِ (٤٩)
```

```english
and also glorify Him during the night and at the receding of the stars. (49)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses conclude the Surah, commanding the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to leave those deniers to their own devices and not to concern himself with their state, but rather to remain patient for the judgment of his Lord and to glorify Him with His praise. Amidst this, there is a reiteration of the threat against them regarding the impending punishment (عذاب واقع) mentioned at the beginning of the Surah—a punishment that none can avert—while adding a further threat of another punishment, lesser than that, for those who do wrong.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَذَرْهُمْ حَتَّى يُلاقُوا يَوْمَهُمُ الَّذِي فِيهِ يُصْعَقُونَ** **So leave them alone until they face their day in which they will be struck by a thunderbolt (45).** The word **ذرهم** (leave them) is a command meaning "abandon them"; it is a verb whose conjugated forms are only used in the future and the imperative. The word **يصعقون** (they will be struck/faint) is derived from *al-is’aq* (الإصعاق), meaning to be put to death, though it is also said to be from *al-sa’q* (الصعق), also meaning to be put to death.

When the Glorified warned the deniers of his call about an impending punishment (عذاب واقع) in which there is no doubt, and then refuted all the excuses they offered—or those they might hypothetically offer—and mentioned that they are so persistent in falsehood that even if they were to witness the clearest sign of truth, they would interpret it away and reject it, He commanded His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to leave them to their own state. This is a metaphorical threat (تهديد كنائي) implying that the punishment will encompass them while they are in this condition.

What is meant by "the day in which they will be struck by a thunderbolt" is the Day of the Blowing of the Trumpet, when everyone in the heavens and the earth will be struck down; this is among the portents of the Hour (أشراط الساعة). The Exalted said: **وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ** **And the Trumpet will be blown, and whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will swoon (39:68).**

This meaning is supported by His saying in the following verse: **يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئاً وَلا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ** **the day when their guile will not avail them anything, nor will they be helped (46).** Indeed, the negation of the efficacy of guile and help is among the specific characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, the day when all secondary causes (الأسباب) fail and the command on that day belongs solely to Allah.

An objection has been raised that only those who are alive on the Day of the Blowing will be struck down, whereas these deniers will not be alive then. The response is that everyone in this world among the living, as well as those in the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — among the dead, will be struck down. If they are not in this world, they are in the intermediate realm (البرزخ).

Furthermore, it is possible that the pronoun in **يُصْعَقُونَ** (they will be struck) refers to those living at that time, and the threat is directed toward the punishment occurring on that day, rather than the specific act of being struck down (الصعقة) itself.

It has been said that it refers to the Day of Badr, but this is far-fetched. It has also been said that it refers to the day of death, but this does not align with the context, which clearly points to the threat mentioned at the beginning of the Surah—namely, the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, not the punishment of the day of death.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا عَذاباً دُونَ ذلِكَ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** **Indeed for those who do wrong there is a punishment besides that, but most of them do not know (47).** It is not improbable that what is meant here is the punishment of the grave (عذاب القبر). His saying, **وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** (but most of them do not know), suggests that among them are those who do know this, yet they persist in their faithlessness (الكفر) and denial out of sheer obstinacy (عناداً). It has also been said that it refers to the Day of Badr, but the conclusion of the verse does not fit that interpretation as well.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا** **So submit patiently to the judgment of your Lord, for indeed you are under Our eyes (48).** This is conjoined to His saying, **فَذَرْهُمْ** (so leave them). The apparent context suggests that the "judgment" (الحكم) refers to His judgment regarding the deniers—granting them respite (الإمهال), dictating their term (الإملاء), and sealing their hearts—and His judgment regarding the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to call to the truth despite the harm he endures for the sake of Allah. Thus, the meaning of His saying, **فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا** (for indeed you are under Our eyes), is: "You are within Our sight; We see you such that nothing of your state is hidden from Us, and We are never heedless of you." The justification of patience with this phrase serves to emphasize the command to be patient and to intensify the address.

It has also been said that the meaning of **فَإِنَّكَ بِأَعْيُنِنا** is: "You are under Our protection and guardianship." Thus, "the eye" (العين) is a metaphor (مجاز) for protection (الحفظ). Perhaps the former meaning is more suited to the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ وَإِدْبارَ النُّجُومِ** **and celebrate the praise of your Lord when you rise, and glorify Him in the night and at the receding of the stars (48-49).** The preposition *bi* (ب) in **بِحَمْدِ** (with praise) denotes accompaniment; that is, "glorify your Lord and declare His transcendence (نزهه) while such glorification is coupled with His praise."

Regarding His saying, **حِينَ تَقُومُ** (when you rise), it has been said to mean rising from sleep. It has also been said to mean rising from the midday nap (القائلة), referring to the Noon Prayer (صلاة الظهر). Others say it means rising from a gathering, or every instance of rising, or rising for the obligatory prayer, or rising for every prayer. It has also been said to refer to the two units (ركعتان) before the obligatory Dawn Prayer. These are seven opinions as mentioned by al-Tabarsi.

His saying, **وَمِنَ اللَّيْلِ فَسَبِّحْهُ** (and glorify Him in the night), means: "And during the night, glorify your Lord therein." This refers to the Night Prayer (صلاة الليل), though it has also been said to refer to the Maghrib and Isha prayers.

His saying, **وَإِدْبارَ النُّجُومِ** (and at the receding of the stars), is said to mean the time when the stars recede—that is, when they disappear due to the light of dawn. This refers to the two units (ركعتان) before the obligatory Dawn Prayer. It has also been said to refer to the obligatory Dawn Prayer itself, or to glorifying the Exalted morning and evening without being heedless of His remembrance.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ** (and celebrate the praise of your Lord when you rise), he said: "For the Night Prayer." Regarding **فَسَبِّحْهُ** (and glorify Him), he said: "The Night Prayer."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated in *Majma’ al-Bayan* from Zurarah, Humran, and Muhammad ibn Muslim, from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليهما السلام).

In the same source, through his chain of narration from Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام), he said: "**أدبار السجود** (after the prostrations) refers to four units (ركعات) after Maghrib, and **إدبار النجوم** (the receding of the stars) refers to two units (ركعتان) before the Dawn Prayer."

**I say:** The latter part of this is narrated in *al-Majma’* from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (عليهما السلام), and by al-Qummi through his chain from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام).

It has been reported through Sunni channels in several narrations that whenever the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) rose from his gathering, he would glorify Allah and praise Him, saying: "It is an expiation for the gathering (كفارة المجلس)." However, these do not clearly appear to be an exegesis (تفسير) of the verse itself.
